content.text: Most organisations begin with a single Azure AD tenant, but several common events introduce a second tenant: a company acquisition where the target has its own Azure AD; a developer who created a personal Azure subscription attached to a Microsoft account tenant; an ISV building a SaaS product that must work across many customer tenants; or an MSP managing Azure resources in client environments.

Each scenario requires a different approach. Tenant boundaries are strict security boundaries — resources, identities, and RBAC assignments in one tenant are invisible to another tenant by default. Cross-tenant access is always explicit and requires action from both sides.

attrs.alt: Three patterns for working across multiple Azure AD tenants: B2B Guest, Multi-Tenant App, and Azure Lighthouse

content.text: Pattern 1: Azure AD B2B Guest Users

B2B (Business-to-Business) collaboration lets you invite users from an external tenant as guests. The guest keeps their home identity (alice@contoso.com) but gets a shadow object in your tenant's directory. You then assign RBAC roles to the guest exactly as you would for an internal user.

# Invite alice@contoso.com to the Fabrikam tenant
az ad invitation create \
  --invited-user-email-address alice@contoso.com \
  --invite-redirect-url https://portal.azure.com \
  --invited-user-display-name "Alice Smith (Contoso)" \
  --send-invitation-message true

# The guest's UPN in the resource tenant takes the form:
# alice_contoso.com#EXT#@fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com

# List all guest users in your tenant
az ad user list --filter "userType eq 'Guest'" \
  --query "[].{DisplayName:displayName,UPN:userPrincipalName,Email:mail}" -o table

# Check a specific guest's redemption status
az ad user show --id "alice_contoso.com#EXT#@fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com" \
  --query "{UPN:userPrincipalName,State:externalUserState,Invited:createdDateTime}" -o table

# Get the guest user's object ID in the resource tenant
GUEST_OID=$(az ad user show \
  --id "alice_contoso.com#EXT#@fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com" \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Assign Contributor on a resource group
az role assignment create \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --assignee-object-id "$GUEST_OID" \
  --assignee-principal-type "User" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<fabrikam-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-shared-project"

# Verify the assignment
az role assignment list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<fabrikam-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-shared-project" \
  --query "[?contains(principalName,'#EXT#')].{Role:roleDefinitionName,User:principalName}" \
  -o table

# Bulk-remove all guest user assignments from a subscription (off-boarding)
az role assignment list --subscription "<fabrikam-sub-id>" \
  --query "[?contains(principalName,'#EXT#')].id" -o tsv | \
  xargs -I{} az role assignment delete --ids {}

When you build a SaaS app used by multiple organisations, register it as a multi-tenant app. Each customer organisation consents to the app, which creates a service principal in their tenant. Your app then acquires access tokens scoped to each customer's tenant independently.

# Register a multi-tenant application
az ad app create \
  --display-name "MyFinanceApp" \
  --sign-in-audience AzureADMultipleOrgs \
  --web-redirect-uris "https://myfinanceapp.com/auth/callback"

APP_ID=$(az ad app list --display-name "MyFinanceApp" --query "[0].appId" -o tsv)

# Create a client secret
az ad app credential reset --id "$APP_ID" --years 2 \
  --query "{ClientId:appId,Secret:password}" -o table

# Add Microsoft Graph delegated permission: User.Read
az ad app permission add \
  --id "$APP_ID" \
  --api 00000003-0000-0000-c000-000000000000 \
  --api-permissions e1fe6dd8-ba31-4d61-89e7-88639da4683d=Scope

# Grant admin consent in the publisher tenant
az ad app permission admin-consent --id "$APP_ID"

# Build the admin consent URL to send to customers
TENANT_ID=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
echo "Admin consent URL for customers:"
echo "https://login.microsoftonline.com/CUSTOMER_TENANT_ID/adminconsent?client_id=${APP_ID}&redirect_uri=https://myfinanceapp.com/auth/callback"

from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
from msgraph import GraphServiceClient

APP_CLIENT_ID     = '<your-app-client-id>'
APP_CLIENT_SECRET = '<your-app-client-secret>'

def get_client_for_tenant(customer_tenant_id: str) -> GraphServiceClient:
    # Get a Graph client scoped to a specific customer tenant
    credential = ClientSecretCredential(
        tenant_id=customer_tenant_id,
        client_id=APP_CLIENT_ID,
        client_secret=APP_CLIENT_SECRET,
    )
    return GraphServiceClient(credential)

# List users in customer tenant A
import asyncio

async def list_users_in_tenant(tenant_id: str):
    client = get_client_for_tenant(tenant_id)
    users = await client.users.get()
    for u in users.value[:5]:
        print(f'  {u.display_name:30}  {u.user_principal_name}')

asyncio.run(list_users_in_tenant('<customer-a-tenant-id>'))

# The same app client ID works for any customer that has consented.
# Only the tenant_id argument changes per customer.

Azure Lighthouse lets an MSP (or internal platform team) manage customer subscriptions without creating accounts in the customer's Azure AD. A Lighthouse delegation projects the customer's subscription ARM scope into the managing tenant. MSP engineers authenticate with their own corporate credentials and see the customer subscription alongside their own subscriptions in the portal.

# Step 1 (MSP tenant): create a group for engineers who will manage customers
az ad group create --display-name "Azure-MSP-Engineers" --mail-nickname "azure-msp-engineers"
MSP_GROUP=$(az ad group show --group "Azure-MSP-Engineers" --query id -o tsv)
MSP_TENANT=$(az account show --query tenantId -o tsv)
echo "MSP Tenant: $MSP_TENANT  Group OID: $MSP_GROUP"

# Step 2 (customer tenant): deploy the Lighthouse delegation ARM template
# Create the template first
cat > /tmp/lighthouse-delegation.json << EOF
{
  "\$schema": "https://schema.management.azure.com/schemas/2018-05-01/subscriptionDeploymentTemplate.json#",
  "contentVersion": "1.0.0.0",
  "parameters": {
    "mspOfferName": {"type": "string"},
    "managedByTenantId": {"type": "string"},
    "principalId": {"type": "string"},
    "roleDefinitionId": {"type": "string"}
  },
  "resources": [{
    "type": "Microsoft.ManagedServices/registrationDefinitions",
    "apiVersion": "2019-09-01",
    "name": "[guid(parameters('mspOfferName'))]",
    "properties": {
      "registrationDefinitionName": "[parameters('mspOfferName')]",
      "managedByTenantId": "[parameters('managedByTenantId')]",
      "authorizations": [{
        "principalId": "[parameters('principalId')]",
        "roleDefinitionId": "[parameters('roleDefinitionId')]",
        "principalIdDisplayName": "MSP Engineering Team"
      }]
    }
  }]
}
EOF

az deployment sub create \
  --location eastus \
  --subscription "<customer-sub-id>" \
  --template-file /tmp/lighthouse-delegation.json \
  --parameters \
    mspOfferName="Contoso MSP Management" \
    managedByTenantId="$MSP_TENANT" \
    principalId="$MSP_GROUP" \
    roleDefinitionId="b24988ac-6180-42a0-ab88-20f7382dd24c"

# Step 3 (MSP tenant): list and access delegated subscriptions
az managedservices assignment list -o table
az account set --subscription "<customer-sub-id>"
az resource list --subscription "<customer-sub-id>" -o table
Switching Tenant Context in the CLI

# Show current active tenant and subscription
az account show --query "{Subscription:name,TenantId:tenantId}" -o table

# List all tenants your account has tokens for
az account tenant list -o table

# Login to a specific tenant (prompts for credentials if no cached token)
az login --tenant "fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com"

# Set active subscription (automatically switches tenant context if needed)
az account set --subscription "<sub-id-in-other-tenant>"

# Use a service principal for non-interactive cross-tenant access
az login \
  --service-principal \
  --username "<app-id>" \
  --password "<secret>" \
  --tenant "<target-tenant-id>"

# Clear all cached tokens and force fresh login
az logout
az account clear
az login
Delete guest account or remove RBAC
B2B Access Reviews and Lifecycle Management

Guest user accounts accumulate over time. Azure AD Access Reviews let you periodically verify that external collaborators still need access and automatically remove accounts that are no longer required.

# Access Reviews are configured via the Azure portal or Microsoft Graph API
# List all guest users and their last sign-in date (identify stale accounts)
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users?\$filter=userType eq 'Guest'&\$select=displayName,userPrincipalName,signInActivity,createdDateTime" \
  --query "value[].{Name:displayName,UPN:userPrincipalName,LastSignIn:signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime,Created:createdDateTime}" \
  -o table

# Find guest users who have not signed in for 90 days
az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users?\$filter=userType eq 'Guest'&\$select=id,displayName,userPrincipalName,signInActivity" \
  --query "value[?signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime < '2026-04-01'].{Name:displayName,UPN:userPrincipalName,LastSignIn:signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime}" \
  -o table

# Remove a stale guest user and all their role assignments
GUEST_UPN="alice_contoso.com#EXT#@fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com"
GUEST_OID=$(az ad user show --id "$GUEST_UPN" --query id -o tsv)

# Remove RBAC assignments first (across all subscriptions)
az role assignment list --all \
  --assignee "$GUEST_OID" \
  --query "[].id" -o tsv | xargs -I{} az role assignment delete --ids {}

# Delete the guest account
az ad user delete --id "$GUEST_OID"
echo "Guest user removed: $GUEST_UPN"

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# offboard-stale-guests.sh — remove guest users inactive > N days

DAYS_THRESHOLD=90
CUTOFF_DATE=$(date -d "-${DAYS_THRESHOLD} days" +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)

echo "Removing guest users not signed in since: $CUTOFF_DATE"

# Get stale guest object IDs
STALE_GUESTS=$(az rest --method GET \
  --url "https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/users?\$filter=userType eq 'Guest'&\$select=id,displayName,signInActivity" \
  --query "value[?signInActivity.lastSignInDateTime < '${CUTOFF_DATE}'].id" -o tsv)

for GUEST_OID in $STALE_GUESTS; do
  UPN=$(az ad user show --id "$GUEST_OID" --query userPrincipalName -o tsv)
  echo "Processing: $UPN ($GUEST_OID)"

  # Remove all RBAC assignments
  ASSIGNMENTS=$(az role assignment list --all --assignee "$GUEST_OID" --query "[].id" -o tsv)
  for ASSIGN_ID in $ASSIGNMENTS; do
    az role assignment delete --ids "$ASSIGN_ID"
    echo "  Removed assignment: $ASSIGN_ID"
  done

  # Delete the guest account
  az ad user delete --id "$GUEST_OID"
  echo "  Deleted guest account."
done

echo "Off-boarding complete."
Some Azure resources can be shared across tenants without requiring guest accounts or app registrations. These mechanisms use resource-level access controls rather than identity-level access.

# Generate a SAS token for cross-tenant blob access
# The SAS is a time-limited signed URL — no AAD identity required in the other tenant

STORAGE_ACCOUNT="mystorageaccount"
CONTAINER="shared-data"
RESOURCE_GROUP="rg-prod"
EXPIRY=$(date -u -d "+7 days" +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%MZ)

# Generate an account SAS with read/list access to the container
SAS_TOKEN=$(az storage account generate-sas \
  --account-name "$STORAGE_ACCOUNT" \
  --resource-types co \
  --services b \
  --permissions rl \
  --expiry "$EXPIRY" \
  --https-only \
  --output tsv)

# Share this URL with the partner tenant — no Azure AD identity needed
BLOB_URL="https://${STORAGE_ACCOUNT}.blob.core.windows.net/${CONTAINER}?${SAS_TOKEN}"
echo "Cross-tenant access URL:"
echo "$BLOB_URL"

# The partner uses it with AzCopy, Python SDK, or REST — no login required
# azcopy copy "${BLOB_URL}" /local/path/ --recursive
Key Vault Cross-Tenant Access via Private Link or IP Rules

# Key Vault can restrict access to specific IP ranges, VNets, or trusted services
# This lets a service in another tenant access the vault if it has the correct network path

# Allow a specific IP range from a partner environment
az keyvault network-rule add \
  --name myvault \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --ip-address "203.0.113.0/24"   # Partner's egress IP block

# Alternatively, allow Azure trusted services (includes cross-tenant trusted scenarios)
az keyvault update \
  --name myvault \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --bypass AzureServices

# The partner service must still present a valid Azure AD token for the vault.
# For cross-tenant app access: create a multi-tenant app, grant it Key Vault Secrets User.
az role assignment create \
  --role "Key Vault Secrets User" \
  --assignee "<partner-app-service-principal-oid-in-your-tenant>" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/myvault"

The Az PowerShell module supports multi-tenant environments through Connect-AzAccount context management. Each context stores a tenant ID, subscription ID, account, and cached token, letting you switch between tenants in the same session.

# Connect to the first tenant (interactive)
Connect-AzAccount -Tenant "contoso.onmicrosoft.com"
$contextA = Get-AzContext
Write-Host "Context A: $($contextA.Tenant.Id) / $($contextA.Subscription.Name)"

# Connect to the second tenant using a service principal
$sp = [PSCredential]::new("<app-id>", (ConvertTo-SecureString "<secret>" -AsPlainText -Force))
Connect-AzAccount -ServicePrincipal -Tenant "fabrikam.onmicrosoft.com" -Credential $sp
$contextB = Get-AzContext
Write-Host "Context B: $($contextB.Tenant.Id) / $($contextB.Subscription.Name)"

# List all available contexts
Get-AzContext -ListAvailable | Format-Table Name, Account, Tenant, Subscription

# Switch between contexts
Select-AzContext -Name $contextA.Name
Get-AzResourceGroup | Format-Table ResourceGroupName, Location

Select-AzContext -Name $contextB.Name
Get-AzResourceGroup | Format-Table ResourceGroupName, Location
Common Pitfalls When Working Across Tenants
AuthorizationFailed on resources you should own
Check az account show; switch tenant context with az account set
Guest cannot access resources despite RBAC assignment
Guest must accept the invitation email before RBAC applies
Service principal not in resource tenant
Multi-tenant app cannot acquire token for customer tenant
Customer admin must grant admin consent to create the SP in their tenant
Wait up to 5 minutes after deploying the delegation ARM template
Guest login succeeds but portal shows no resources
Check customer tenant's Conditional Access policies for guest exceptions
Multiple az login sessions overwrite context
CLI commands hit wrong tenant after re-login
Use az account set --subscription explicitly; verify with az account show

Working with multiple Azure AD tenants requires choosing the right access pattern for the situation. B2B guest users suit individuals collaborating across organisations — the guest authenticates with their home identity and is assigned RBAC in the resource tenant. Multi-tenant app registrations suit SaaS products that need to act in many customer tenants — customers consent once, a service principal is created in their tenant, and the app acquires per-tenant tokens using the same client ID. Azure Lighthouse suits MSPs managing customer subscriptions — subscriptions are projected into the managing tenant via ARM delegation, with no accounts created in the customer's Azure AD. The Azure CLI's az login --tenant and az account set commands let you navigate between tenant contexts interactively.
